title: Quantum computing in transition - Nature Biotechnology
text:
Download PDF

Hybrid quantum–classical computers will achieve quantum advantage in biotechnology, bridging the time until a full quantum computer becomes available.

The year 2025 was declared the International Year of Quantum Science and Technology by the United Nations General Assembly, celebrating 100 years of quantum science. Yet, amid this excitement surrounding quantum computers and their promise to revolutionize medicine, chemistry and biotechnology across the globe, an important reality is often overlooked: quantum advantage — the state in which a quantum computer solves a practical, real-world problem faster or more efficiently than the most powerful classical supercomputer — is unlikely to emerge from a fully quantum machine at first. It will instead come from hybrid quantum–classical computing platforms that combine the strengths of quantum processors with the scale and reliability of classical computing.In fact, there have already been exciting developments in the application of quantum computing to biotechnology achieved by hybrid quantum–classical systems. Researchers have successfully encoded and analyzed the entire genome of the hepatitis D virus on IBM quantum hardware1. Results from the international Quantum for Bio (Q4Bio) challenge showed that hybrid quantum–classical methods can contribute to molecular simulation and drug-discovery problems, including simulation of protein complexes over 12,000 atoms — the largest known simulations of biologically meaningful molecules2. In January this year, IBM predicted that 2026 will be the inflection point where we will see a true quantum advantage using hybrid systems.Current quantum computers are powerful but incomplete tools, and biological systems are complex. Quantum computers are not yet able to address biological challenges independently and at scale. Qubits, the fundamental unit of information in a quantum computer, are error-prone — vibrations or slight variations in temperature or electromagnetic field can cause loss of coherence. Current quantum computers are noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices: they have dozens or a few hundred qubits but are not error-corrected and introduce errors into their operations.Hybrid systems use quantum error mitigation, reducing the impact of noise on quantum computations without requiring full error correction. Classical computers and quantum computers are tightly coupled in iterative loops where only small parts of the computation are performed on the quantum hardware and the classical computer is used to handle errors, perform optimization and process data. These systems act as a bridge to achieving quantum advantage, but they are not likely to scale well to the number of qubits required for use cases that are projected to eventually benefit most from quantum computing, such as molecular simulation and drug design, or protein or RNA folding.Error correction techniques, by contrast, achieve physical correction of quantum states while maintaining the phase relationship between quantum states. Combining multiple physical qubits into a logical qubit detects and corrects errors without directly measuring the qubits’ states. However, a critical challenge here is that the correction itself adds more qubits and more error-prone operations to the system; therefore, the base error rate needs to be below a certain threshold to make error correction advantageous. Google’s Willow chip achieves this sub-threshold error rate3. As more qubits are added with this type of correction, the logical error rate is suppressed exponentially — hinting at possible routes to building a large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computer, with estimates for realization as soon as 2030.While researchers pursue the long-term goal of fault-tolerant quantum computers, the pharmaceutical industry has adopted a more pragmatic perspective, extracting useful insights from the quantum technologies available now and investing heavily in collaborations with quantum hardware providers so as to get ready to make use of a full-scale quantum computer once it becomes available. As these companies have access to sensitive intellectual property and clinical data, they must also consider the security risk. Sensitive encrypted data can be intercepted and stored using a ‘harvest now, decrypt later’ strategy, and once a sufficiently powerful quantum computer becomes available it will be able to be deciphered. Resource estimates for breaking RSA-2048, a commonly used encryption scheme, have dropped from around 20 million physical qubits to potentially fewer than 100,000 with new algorithms and architectures4,5. Genomic and health data do not expire and are uniquely exposed to this threat: while the data themselves are often available, they are anonymized and the information needed to deanonymize them is encrypted. Data collected today and decrypted in 2035 will be as sensitive as on the day they were created — maybe more so, given that genomic analysis techniques will be more advanced. Post-quantum cryptographic standards have been available6 since 2024, introducing encryption methods that are designed to be secure against attacks by future quantum computers. Biological databases need to develop timelines for adoption to protect sensitive data.Biotechnology already occupies a central role in economic competitiveness, healthcare innovation and national security. As these sectors converge in the realm of quantum computing, policymakers will face questions concerning infrastructure, workforce development, intellectual property and equitable access. Early engagement will be essential to ensure that scientific opportunity translates into societal benefit.Technologies that are strategically sensitive and capital-intensive tend to concentrate in a limited number of hands, raising the possibility that quantum advantage may not be evenly distributed across the global scientific and industrial landscape. The institutions with early access — a handful of large pharmaceutical companies, national labs and well-funded universities in the United States, Europe and China — will gain first-mover advantages in identification of structurally difficult drug target identification, for example. This means that the first wave of quantum-enabled drug discoveries will almost certainly address diseases in areas where the institutions with quantum access have financial incentives to direct their computing. Neglected tropical diseases and antimicrobial resistance in low-income settings will not be early beneficiaries of quantum-accelerated biology. This is not an inevitable outcome, but it is the default trajectory unless explicitly countered by global initiatives such as the one proposed by UNESCO, which aims to address this ‘quantum divide’ by providing an open platform for dialog, cooperation and capacity building.Quantum computing is unlikely to transform biotechnology in a single leap. Instead, its impact is emerging through hybrid systems that combine quantum and classical strengths, delivering incremental advantage while full fault tolerance remains out of reach. The challenge is not only to build quantum advantage, but to ensure it arrives in a form that can be securely, responsibly and equitably used.
